data.title: Invesco Global Allocation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The fund's developed ex-US tactical factor equities were a positive contributor

The fund's allocation to Invesco International Developed Dynamic Multifactor ETF (IMFL) (IMFL) was one of the top contributors to relative return for the quarter, driven by its cyclical factor positioning.

2 The fund's macro framework experienced a regime change in the fourth quarter

The fund was positioned for a contraction regime and shifted to a recovery regime in November, driven by improving growth expectations. In our view, the transition does not represent the start of a new cycle, but an extension of the existing cycle.

3 Tactical foreign exchange activity detracted from relative performance

Tactical foreign exchange activity was the largest detractor from relative performance. Allocations to US and emerging market equities also detracted.

Manager perspective and outlook

• Global economic growth held up in the fourth quarter and markets ended higher despite volatility. Risk assets rallied early but stalled in November amid apparent uncertainty about US Federal Reserve (Fed) policy and artificial intelligence (AI) related valuation concerns. December saw renewed strength in US, European and emerging (ex-China) equities. Credit markets faced isolated pressures from bankruptcies and regional banks but still had gains.

• Geopolitics appeared to influence investor sentiment. US/China tensions rose over rare earth minerals and tariffs before easing after a temporary trade deal. The US government shutdown appeared to add uncertainty as data releases were paused. Central banks were active: the Fed cut rates twice and raised its 2026 growth outlook, the Bank of England lowered rates and the European Central Bank raised growth and inflation forecasts.

• We expect growth to reaccelerate in 2026, supported by lower US policy rates and more fiscal spending across major economies. Private sector balance sheets have remained a tailwind. We believe Fed easing should support US growth later in the year, while less aggressive easing abroad points in our view to a weaker dollar and improving opportunities outside the US, including emerging market local debt. In Europe, we believe fiscal expansion and real wage gains should bolster activity. Given elevated US valuations, we favor broadening exposures to cyclicals.

Portfolio positioning

Invesco Solutions' macro regime framework remained in a recovery regime entering the first quarter. Global growth has been broadening, with improvements in non-US developed markets, and inflation has continued to moderate across regions. This environment should in our view support broadening of equity performance across sectors and regions.

We maintain overall portfolio risk above the benchmark , with an overweight in equities relative to fixed income, tilting toward value and small- and mid-capitalizations. Regional equity exposure is in line with the benchmark. In fixed income, we maintain an overweight in credit risk and a neutral duration stance.

Within equities , we maintain overweight exposure in cyclical sectors, value and small- and mid-cap stocks because these equity segments carry higher operating leverage and historically tend to outperform during cyclical rebounds. Accordingly, we favor sectors such as financials, industrials, materials and energy at the expense of health care, consumer staples, utilities and technology. We kept the fund's regional composition in line with the benchmark given mixed signals among key drivers of relative performance between US, developed ex-US and emerging market equities. On one hand, US earnings momentum has continued to outperform other markets. On the other hand, our expectations for US dollar depreciation, driven by narrowing yield differentials and positive global growth surprises, favor international equities. As a result, we express no active views in the fund's regional exposures at this stage.

In fixed income , we maintain an overweight in credit. However, with spreads near all-time lows, we believe the case for taking additional credit risk is limited to harvesting higher yields. As a result, we seek diversification across high-yield, leveraged loans and emerging markets dollar debt, while underweighting investment grade credit and sovereign fixed income. Our bearish positioning on the US dollar also favors emerging market local debt and global fixed income, currency unhedged, over core US fixed income. We maintain an overweight in Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) relative to nominal Treasuries following the transition to a recovery regime, which has historically been accompanied by rising expectations for future inflation.

Performance highlights

Invesco Global Allocation Fund Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) had a positive return in the fourth quarter and outperformed its blended benchmark, the Custom Invesco Global Allocation Index.

The fund was positioned for a contraction regime and shifted to a recovery regime in November, driven by improving growth expectations. In our view, the transition does not represent the beginning of a new cycle, but rather an extension of the existing cycle, given tight labor markets and credit spreads between Treasury and non-Treasury sectors being near all-time lows.

Following the transition of our macro framework to a cyclical recovery, we implemented several changes to Invesco Global Allocation Fund. We increased overall portfolio risk above the benchmark, moving to an overweight in equities relative to fixed income and tilting toward value and small- and mid-capitalization stocks. Our increased equity exposure -- particularly the shift toward mid and small cap stocks -- has allowed us to rotate into areas of the market that we believe offer more attractive valuations. As part of this repositioning, we reduced exposure to expensive defensive sectors, which have been trading at an average forward P/E of about 19.5x. We then reallocated toward more historically economically sensitive (cyclical) sectors, where valuations appear considerably lower, with an average forward P/E closer to 8.5x.

In fixed income, we increased credit risk to an overweight relative to the benchmark and reduced duration back to neutral following the decline in bond yields. We continued to underweight the US dollar, driven by narrowing yield differentials relative to the rest of the world and positive economic data surprises outside the US.

Contributors to performance

From a top-down allocation perspective, the primary contributors to relative return were an underweight in international fixed income relative to the benchmark and an allocation to developed ex-US tactical factor equities.

Detractors from performance

From a top-down allocation perspective, the primary detractors from relative return were allocations to US large-cap and emerging market equities.

The fund's foreign exchange activity was a detractor from relative performance.

data.title: Janus Henderson Small-Mid Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 2.47% and the Russell 2500™ Value Index returned 3.15%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the information technology and industrials sectors detracted from relative performance, while stock selection in the materials and real estate sectors contributed.

Outlook

We remain confident in our disciplined value investment approach even as we see potential sources of market volatility.

Investment environment

• Stocks rose on positive earnings news and expectations for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts. The Fed followed up a September rate cut with two 25 basis-point (bp) rate cuts, in October and December, respectively.

• Given the protracted government shutdown, economic data was sparse except for company commentary and private investment surveys, which pointed to a slowly improving economic environment.

• While investors paid more attention to fundamentals relative to earlier in the year, we continued to see pockets of outperformance by lower-quality stocks, as non-earners continued to outperform.

Portfolio review

Relative performance was hindered by our position in Bath & Body Works (BBWI). The specialty retailer reported disappointing revenue growth and reduced guidance, reflecting both cautious spending by its consumers as well as some operational issues that need to be addressed. Despite an inexpensive valuation, we have meaningfully reduced our holdings given our concerns over poor execution by company management, and because we believe the next iteration of the company's turnaround will take considerable time.

Detractors in the industrials sector included Janus International Group (JBI), a manufacturer of industrial doors and storage units for the self-storage providers. Demand for self-storage space has remained anemic due to sluggish home sales and slower housing turnover. Against this backdrop, Janus International reported disappointing quarterly results. We added to the position on weakness.

Owens Corning (OC) was another detractor. We invested in the roofing and building products supplier because of its attractive valuation, which we believed amply priced in the risk of continued weakness in new home starts. However, a less severe storm season also resulted in fewer roof repairs, further dampening the company's revenue growth. Given near-term uncertainty for the business, we exited the position.

Kirby Corporation (KEX) was a positive contributor. Kirby transports petroleum and petrochemical products. It also operates an engine overhaul business, which has seen strong growth due to the rapid buildout in U.S. data center capacity. After underperforming in the third quarter owing to weaker petrochemical volumes, the stock moved higher in the fourth quarter, as the company became viewed as an AI beneficiary. We continue to hold a sizeable position in Kirby.

Globus Medical (GMED) was a relative contributor in the healthcare sector. The medical device-maker delivered strong execution and improved profitability, supported by synergies from recent acquisitions. Robust sales of spinal products resulted in better-than-expected third-quarter earnings growth and an improved outlook.

Information technology company Teradyne (TER) was another positive contributor, as continued rapid investment AI and other advanced computing drove demand for its semiconductor testing equipment. The company is also priced at a lower valuation relative to other AI-related stocks, and we believe it provides an attractive avenue for capitalizing on the AI buildout.

The portfolio ended the quarter overweight in the financials, information technology, materials, and energy sectors. It was underweight in the communication services, industrials, healthcare, real estate, utilities, consumer staples, and consumer discretionary sectors.

Manager outlook

We remain cautiously optimistic about the market in 2026. The economy continues to grow at a steady pace, and we may see modest improvement in cyclical sectors such as transportation, industrials, and materials. While weaker employment growth and above-average inflation have pressured consumer spending by lower-income households, this slowdown has been offset by resilient spending from higher-income households benefiting from equity price appreciation. We also see potential for additional Fed rate cuts and fiscal stimulus that could provide a tailwind for economic growth.

At the same time, many companies remain cautious in their hiring and capital spending plans. Valuations in the equity market do not appear cheap by historical standards, but we have continued to find areas of opportunity, such as in the healthcare sector. Recently, the market has experienced increased volatility around the AI trade: on some days, excitement around AI drives market returns, while on other days investor concerns lead to a recalibration. We would expect this dynamic to continue as we head into 2026.

As always, we are on the lookout for opportunities to use volatility to our advantage as we seek out high-quality opportunities yet to be recognized by other investors. We remain focused on investing in well-managed, profitable companies with low debt levels that we believe are undervalued by the market. In our view, such companies can outperform regardless of the economic backdrop.

We thank you for your continued co-investment in the Janus Henderson Small-Mid Cap Value Fund (JVSIX).

data.title: Fidelity Mid Cap Index Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Performance Review

The fund finished the fourth quarter in line with the 0.16% gain of the benchmark, the Russell Midcap® Index. Security lending income and security misweights due to certain benchmark securities that the fund is restricted from buying were additive to overall Fund performance for the period. The performance variance between futures held by the fund (to equitize cash and accruals) and that of the index also had positive impacts. Efficient trading and implementation strategies helped limit fund transaction costs while replicating the exposure and characteristics of the benchmark.

Results for U.S. mid-cap stocks were roughly flat in the fourth quarter, according to the Russell index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. Stocks entered October on an uptrend, but with the federal government in a shutdown that would last until November 12. On October 29, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points at its second consecutive meeting. The index returned -0.83% for the month. Mid-caps rallied modestly in November (+1.27%), despite the continued shutdown and a brief mid-month sell-off. Meanwhile, the Fed cut rates by another quarter point on December 10. The index returned -0.28% for the month and achieved a 10.60% gain for the year and a 30.09% increase since the low on April 8.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented health care sector (+6%), which particularly shined in November. Materials (+3%) and industrials (+1%) also delivered modest gains. Information technology, which represented 12% of the index in Q4, gained 3%, led by technology hardware & equipment (+9%) companies. All other groups lagged the index. The growth-oriented communication services sector returned -5%, hampered by media & entertainment stocks (-6%). The more-defensive real estate (-5%) and utilities (-3%) sectors each lost ground. Consumer discretionary (-2%) and consumer staples (-1%) also delivered weak results, and energy (-2%) was held back by a sharp decline in the price of oil. Meanwhile, financials (0%) delivered a flat return, reflecting a strong showing by banks (+6%) but weakness among financial services (-2%).

Regardless of the market environment, we continue to apply a disciplined investment process across all our strategies, relying on highly skilled professionals and robust investment infrastructure. Investment performance is the foundation of our value proposition for shareholders. This is true of our comprehensive suite of low-cost index funds. We expect our index funds to deliver low tracking difference, which is the difference in a fund's performance to that of its stated benchmark. We also seek to minimize tracking error, which measures the volatility of these return differences over a period of time. Whether it's through solid trading techniques for funds that replicate an index or our optimization techniques, when necessary, we are focused on delivering returns in line with benchmark performance. data.title: Nebius: Catch This AI Dip With A Safety Net
data.description:
Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) is firing on all cylinders, yet the stock is stuck in a trading range around $100. The AI data center company is lining up a path to considerable revenue growth beyond consensus forecasts. My investment thesis remain ultra Bullish on the stock, especially on a dip to the recent lows of the range between $75 and $130.

Meta Safety Net

In a major surprise, Meta Platforms (META) not only signed up to utilize substantial data center capacity for the first large-scale deployments of Nvidia's Vera Rubins in early 2027 from Nebius, but the social media giant also agreed to absorb any unused capacity. Nebius now has the freedom to grow at a rapid pace knowing Meta will provide a safety net.

The contract requires Meta to purchase $12 billion in AI infrastructure capacity over a 5-year team for an average annual revenue generation of $2.4 billion. The company agreed to spend another $15 billion on unused capacity for a total contract value of up to $27 billion, or $5.4 billion annually.

Nebius is fully booked for up to $30 billion in sales to Meta, including $3 billion from last year, with the optionality to find better AI Cloud deals on half of the capacity. The company already targeted ending 2026 with $7 to $9 billion in ARR and this quickly grows the ARR base by up to 50% with the ability to boost sales by signing up 3rd-party AI cloud customers at higher rates than the bare metal requirements of Meta.

The key here is that Nebius can now move forwards with the plans for additional data center capacity with a goal of reaching 3 GW in contracted power by the end of 2026. When adding the Microsoft (MSFT) deal with up to $19.4 billion in revenue, Nebius now has nearly $50 billion in commitments from Meta and Microsoft while acknowledging that half of the 2026 ARR commitment is from other customers.

On the Q4'25 earnings call, CRO Marc Boroditsky highlighted how the $8 billion ARR goal wasn't dependent on this massive Meta deal and the prior mega deals are only about $4 billion in ARR:

Our 2026 ARR target is not dependent on any new mega deals. As we bring on our planned additional capacity, combined with the already strong pipeline, and our go-to-market plans, we are very confident in our ability to deliver our '26 ARR target.

Since Nebius hasn't announced contracts with AI labs or enterprises, the market isn't really focusing on the non-hyperscaler ARR base is at least 50% of the current business.

Big Upside

The consensus analyst estimates have Nebius hitting 2027 revenues of just $9.7 billion. The number is triple the $3.3 billion estimate for 2026, but the amounts appear very conservative considering where the ARR is forecast to land to end 2026 plus the addition of this major Meta contract.

The ending 2026 ARR goal of $7 to $9 billion at the midpoint sets Nebius up for this revenue goal as a starting point. The Meta deal immediately kicks up ARR to the $9.5 to 11.5 billion range plus the overflow revenues.

Of course, the big question is the full timing of the $3 billion annual safety net. Either way, Nebius has targeted ending 2026 with 800 MW to 1 GW of connected power, suggesting the $9 billion ARR target amounts to 1 GW of connected power prior to the Meta deal, which doesn't start until early 2027.

In essence, the Meta deal alone likely gets Nebius to the $15 billion revenue estimate for 2028. The company can sell additional capacity to other customers or take the overflow Meta capacity and upsell to customers looking for more valuable AI cloud services.

Nebius has a goal of reaching 2030 with 5+ GW of infrastructure capacity from Nvidia (NVDA) alone. Just using the rough estimates of $9 billion in ARR per GW of connected power, the company has a goal for at least $45 billion of revenues by 2030.

The stock has see-sawed with Nebius following up the Meta deal with a large convertible debt offering. The company ended up raising $4.3 billion in gross proceeds to fund the data center expansion.

The market has reached the point where new contracts mean additional capital spending, which has become a huge negative. Nebius had just raised $2 billion via the investment from Nvidia, so the company has $6.3 billion in funding right around the big Meta deal is seen in a negative light.

Nebius will soon start throwing off a lot of cash. The stock only has a market cap of $25 billion and the ~$8 billion in ARR to start 2026 will produce up to $2 billion in EBIT profits with positive cash flows considering most of the costs are depreciation of GPUs at a 5-year schedule now.

The AI data center company has plans for up to 30% EBIT margins leading to a an EBIT goal of $13.5 billion based on this above revenue target of $45 billion on the 5 GW of capacity. The revenue target is double the current market cap and the company has limited interest expenses, along with assets like ClickHouse to pay down debt in the future.

Unlike the other neoclouds, Nebius has limited debt for the scale of the business, considering Q4 spent $2 billion on capex and the company had nearly $1 billion in operating cash flows. The company ended 2025 in a small net debt position and just raised $4.3 billion in converts to cover a large portion of the capital spending in 2026.

The biggest risk is that AI infrastructure overspending becomes an issue and Nebius spends upwards of $20 billion on capex this year while customers pull back from planned spending. A scenario where Meta is able to void being a safety net would become very detrimental for the stock.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Nebius continues humming along signing massive hyperscaler deals while the business isn't even reliant on Meta or Microsoft. Investors should use on going weakness in the stock to buy Nebius with big 2027 revenue upside already contracted and that stock not reflecting this reality.

data.title: Can Traditional Data Services Stocks Still Succeed Amid AI Disruption?
data.description:
Data services software stocks have been under pressure due to the growing capabilities of artificial intelligence. But Ram Sampath, VP for Portfolio Research with TD Asset Management, says some of these “invisible giants” can still succeed in this environment.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Our feature guest today has characterized big data services firms as the invisible giants of the markets. But what's next for these names, as investors fear that artificial intelligence can disrupt all those business models? Joining us now to discuss is Ram Sampath, VP for Portfolio Research with TD Asset Management. Great to have you back on the show.

Ram Sampath: Good to be back, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: All right, we're going to do some catch up now. The last time we had you on the show, we were talking about these invisible giants as a key part of the market, information services companies. Let's talk about some of the challenges now being presented by AI.

Ram Sampath: Are they called as invisible giants? I think they're more so invisible ghosts, I think, at this point of time. If you think about what's happened over the last six months, the stocks are down around 45%. And in the last three months, year to date, they're down around 25%. And part of the biggest reason is AI.

So AI has kind of started to disrupt the space in different ways. And the reason why is that these invisible giants, which we called before, had a lot of data. And what AI does best is summarize and analyze data as much as they can.

So we thought that these were going to be winners because you had a lot of data. You can just input something on top of your data and give a more precise and fast and accurate result. But it turns out, that's not the case. People are looking at Pareto's rule, for example. So they're willing to get 80% of the answer at 20% of the time. And they are willing to sacrifice that 20% of an answer, which might be wrong.

And so you have all of this public data, which is, again, not a hundred years old, which might be five years old. And things are changing very quickly. And that is part of the reason why these stocks have been underperforming.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about the pace of AI development. I think when people were first able to access this technology, a year or two back, on their phone, they weren't that impressed. They were getting a lot of wrong answers. They thought, this isn't going to play out. But things are changing pretty quick.

Ram Sampath: It is. If we go back two years, you can systematically think about what you would be querying AI. So I would at least say what I was doing. I said, hey, AI, write me a good story which I can read for the next 10 minutes. Write me a poem, or something like that. Or tell me a joke, right?

Things are changing. Now students are using AI for them to analyze information and give them a summary of complex math, which they are not able to understand, or complex physics like, AI is able to break that down.

The other part is, I'm going on vacation soon. So I asked AI, can you plan a trip to Spain and Portugal? And then it actually gave me a really, really good summary. And then I decided, wow, it's actually able to plan better than I would have planned by doing 10 Google searches, right?

So things are changing very, very quickly. And the way it has gone is it was at this wow phase last two years ago. And now it's kind of become an everyday phase. I cannot live without doing AI. That comes back to the point when I think about Google Maps or GPS, people were using GPS at one point. And then when Google Maps took over, people started using Google Maps. And they were like, wow, it can actually come on a smartphone. Now I don't need to have this massive GPS device for me to go from one place to another.

And then two or three years later, it just became an everyday sensation. So no one really talked about it. So right now we are in this everyday phase. Everyone is digesting AI on a daily basis. And I think there's more room to go in terms of improvement.

Greg Bonnell: The last GPS system that was stolen in my car was around the same time that I just realized I had it on my phone and I don't need to keep giving the criminals GPS for them to lift out of my car. So things have moved pretty quickly. And I said, I do the same thing all the time. I pick up my phone, get the map going, throw the phone there, drive along. So this is going to leave some companies exposed. Which ones are most exposed?

Ram Sampath: So again, I think you spoke about necessity being the mother of invention. Innovation is key here. If you have data, you thought you were king before. But you're not the kingmaker anymore. So just having the data doesn't give you the right to win in this game. Having a workflow makes you the right to win, right?

So the ones which are at risk at the top level are the ones who are not innovating, just have the data, and just have just like an AI layer on top of it. And basically, if you query, it will give you an answer. It will not give you a holistic point of view. And you won't have a research assistant helping you to understand and assimilate information faster than they can.

So those are the companies which are poised to lose in the long frame. But in general, if you have a workflow, if you have a complete algorithm, if you have a research assistant who is telling you, maybe that is not the right way to think about this, I think those are the stocks which are expected to benefit the most.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about some of those companies that are better positioned in this environment. The other ones had data, maybe a little AI layer laying on top of it. Who can actually take advantage of this moment?

Ram Sampath: Correct. So there are two ways to think about this. So let's think about, let's say Thomson, for instance. So they have this massive 150-year legal database, which is really important. It has access to a multitude of cases, which have been either won or lost. It tells you what sort of case has been won based on which argument, et cetera.

So those are expected to benefit, only if you have a good layer on top of it. The reason why I say it is because, as much as you know whatever cases have been won, you need someone to tell you, okay, these cases have been won because of the arguments which have been made. And the draft has to be proper.

So if you have a co-counsel or an assistant assisting you, you will probably benefit the most. The reason why I say Thomson is legal is very finicky. You cannot come up with a wrong argument in court. You could potentially get disbarred. Same thing with accounting too, right? So if you're filing for taxes, if you have AI file the wrong taxes or submit the wrong forms, then there is obviously IRS going to knock at the door, CRA going to knock at the door and say, you actually evaded taxes. So things where the moat is more defensible, where you cannot go wrong, when I said last time, 80/20--

Greg Bonnell: I'm not willing to risk a 20% error rate on my taxes.

Ram Sampath: I would rather pay the extra incremental $100 to make sure that I don't get jailed or whatever. So those are the things. You also have to take into consideration the software part of the market. So can we move away from something like an Excel, a Word? It's so ingrained in your work system that it's very, very, very, very hard to displace something like a Microsoft.

All they're doing is they're incrementally giving more products. So think of it like your mobile phone, your touch phones, when it came out in 2008 or '09. The way you would think about it is more and more apps become compatible. And so as more apps become compatible, Microsoft (MSFT) kind of becomes like a major player here.

Greg Bonnell: As we put all that together, how should investors be thinking about the space right now?

Ram Sampath: When we are thinking about AI, let's not just purely think about AI as being disrupting these companies completely. Whenever there is a shift in technology, things are not very fast. Things go down at a slow pace.

AI should also be viewed as something very similar. The reason I say that is, yes, it has encroached into your day-to-day activities as of where we are today. But you go back in history, streaming didn't change people going to movies.

If you think about Amazon (AMZN), online retail was the biggest change, which everyone had. Something could be delivered at your doorstep. But people still visited malls or went to the grocery store. That still happened. Technology is changing. But the pace at which things are changing are quick. But eventually, things will take over.

The one thing for you to understand within AI is, is it disrupting the flow of work today? If it is, then that's a company which you don't want to be associated with. If it's not disrupting today, look at the second and third degree. Can it happen over the next 10 to 15 years? Then answer the question and say, okay, this is going to be a slow bleed. But there is a mode for the next 10-15 years.

data.title: Ominous Action (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Last week's moves in the S&P500 (SPY) followed a familiar pattern - the Monday bounce, the Thursday drop (the 9th in a row), and the rush for the exits on Friday in fear of a weekend Iran war escalation. Unfortunately, the large gap higher on Monday meant the promising technicals I outlined last weekend only aligned on Friday, and by that time, support levels had no effect.

This week's article looks ahead at April and Q2 and highlights a worrying shift in the bigger picture technicals that support my 2026 outlook of a large correction that could eventually target 5700.

S&P 500 Monthly and Quarterly

Some technicians add sophisticated indicators and signals to their charts, but I find the simple stuff works best, and the price action tells the most reliable story.

The price action on the quarterly chart has shifted bearish as the Q1 bar made news highs that were rejected, and it is now below the Q4 low, effectively "engulfing" its range. There are still two sessions to go until the March close, so anything could happen, but it looks very likely to close weak, and it is comparable to the patterns at the 2025 top and the 2022 top.

The monthly chart tells a similar story as the March bar confirms the slow action in January and February was due to distribution. Further lows are expected in April and Q2, although April is generally a bullish month, so a recovery should set up.

6147-200 is major support at the breakout level. The 20SMA is at 6233.

Resistance is 6775 and 7002.

A DeMARK exhaustion count completed in February and is having an effect. This signal has led to a drop of at least 10% on the last 3 occasions.

S&P 500 Weekly

The weekly chart has a familiar bearish formation, as for the fourth time in a row, lower highs and lower lows were made, and the close was at new 2026 lows. 6520-50 support has been broken, as has the 50SMA.

There isn't much compelling support now until 6147-6174 (the 38% Fib retrace).

One small comfort comes from the weekly Demark exhaustion signal progressing to bar 9 (of 9) next week. A reaction is often seen on bars 8 or 9, and the last comparable downside signal completed right at the April '25 lows. The signal before that came right at the October '22 bottom (again on bar 8). However, it's not a green light to take big risks, and bar 9 could drop significantly before stabilization.

6520-50 is initial resistance, then 6764 and the underside of the 20SMA.

S&P 500 Daily

The S&P 500 has made a symmetrical "rounded top," and the significant distribution volume around 6800 is ominous.

The 200SMA was convincingly broken and held as resistance last week. They say nothing good happens under the 200SMA, but that is not true. Ask anyone who bought last April and May. Actually, the best moves tend to happen in the very early stages of a reversal when volatility is still high.

The daily chart only has minor supports at 6343 and 6200 before the major 6147 level.

A lot of the important action has taken place in overnight trading, and it is worth keeping an eye on the intraday channel on the 24hr chart.

Monday's rally moved from the bottom of the channel to the top. Interestingly, it was the same size as the rally on Monday, 9th, a move again triggered by Trump claiming the war would be over "very soon."

Drivers/Events

Monday's futures sell-off and the impending 48-hour deadline for Iran to open the Strait of Hormuz created a very dangerous situation for markets. I was pretty sure Trump would say something to avert disaster, but couldn't think what it would be.

Right on cue, he issued a statement claiming there were "major points of agreement" in talks with Iran and that the ultimatum would be extended to 5 days.

Did he make that up so he wouldn't have to follow through with the threat and create global chaos?

Are talks even taking place?

It's hard to tell. Iran claims they aren't, but this may be a front or a negotiating tactic. They surely want a peace deal, but have some leverage and won't cave quickly.

Markets are clearly skeptical and are calling Trump's bluff. Nothing he said after Monday's original statement had much effect. Even extending the ultimatum from 5 days to 10 days made little difference.

An extended deadline at least buys some time, and perhaps better news comes out over the weekend or next week. A concession, or action from Trump (such as pulling out some military instead of amassing it), would be a step forward and show he is serious about pursuing peace. Iran or markets do not trust vague comments.

Next week is jobs week, and Chair Powell is due to speak on Monday. I'm not convinced the hawkish shift by central banks will be as significant as markets hastily priced in (3 hikes from the ECB are now priced in this year). Indeed, the market response has done a lot of the tightening for them.

Weak jobs numbers would present the Fed with a dilemma, and they will have to decide if inflation or the labour market takes priority. However, that is probably an issue for later this year. For now, the safest course of action would be to pause, buy time, and hope the Iran war resolves soon.

Probable Moves

The higher timeframe charts have developed reversal patterns, and the bearish view from my 2026 outlook seems to be playing out. That eventually calls for a move to 5700ish, but could take all year, and I am loosely basing my expectations on the last Trump mid-term year of 2018. That started with a Q1 drop of nearly 12%, recovered to a brief new high, and then fell around 20% into the end of the year.

Next week looks set for more of the same kind of action as recent weeks. That means there could be a Monday/Tuesday bounce to lower highs with 6651, and 6520-50 resistance may cap for weakness into the end of the week.

There has not yet been a significant spike in the VIX and a capitulation of 3-5%, so that may be setting up and could take the S&P500 to the 6147-74 area. I plan on buying there for a Q2 recovery, although caution is warranted, and I don't intend on buying for the long-term until 5700 is reached.

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 2033-2036 Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, although upward momentum slowed in the final quarter of the year.
  • The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI, and the Fed's interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December.
  • Healthcare, a laggard for much of 2025, was the top-performing sector in the S&P 500 Index during Q4.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and slightly trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 50.58% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 21.67% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net) and a 27.75% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) also detracted. Stock selection in healthcare, communication services, industrials, and consumer discretionary proved disappointing for the quarter. Conversely, International Growth Fund (John Hancock) contributed on a relative basis, largely due to security selection in the financials sector. U.S. Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by stock selection in IT and industrials. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.

data.title: Virtus Zevenbergen Technology Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Perspective: Same Song, Different Verse

U.S. equity markets ended 2025 by notching three straight years of double-digit gains, a feat last seen in the late 1990s. On trend with recent history, strong corporate profits and AI momentum powered through a softening labor market and noisy inflation signals. Positive benchmark returns were again attributable to relatively few companies, concentrated in mega-cap platforms and AI infrastructure beneficiaries, with modest index gains masking sharp reversals in momentum factors and retail-favorite stock baskets.

Fund Performance

The Virtus Zevenbergen Technology Fund returned 2.32% (Class INST) during the quarter, trailing the S&P North American Technology Sector Index's 3.39% return. Unfavorable positioning in industrials (aerospace) and consumer discretionary (e-commerce) was offset slightly by positive contributions in technology (semiconductors and application software).

Alphabet (GOOG) and Micron Technology (MU) were the largest contributors to performance in the quarter.

  • > Technology conglomerate Alphabet reported above-consensus results, with double-digit growth across its online search, video entertainment, and web services businesses. In addition to the positive earnings report, investors rejoiced over Alphabet's progress on artificial intelligence services. Its Gemini AI assistant has attracted 650 million users, a sevenfold increase from a year ago, and improved its ranking on industry performance evaluations. We believe Alphabet can preserve its perception as an AI leader through further product capability advancements.
  • > Shares of memory chipmaker Micron surged on favorable pricing dynamics for storage semiconductors due to voracious demand for AI "building blocks." Although the company is investing heavily in manufacturing facilities to ease supply conditions, additional production capacity will not likely become available until 2027. Given the beneficial market environment, management issued optimistic commentary and stronger-than-expected financial guidance, compelling positive revisions to analyst earnings estimates.

Meta Platforms (META) and Oracle (ORCL) were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • > Social media conglomerate Meta underperformed, despite strong Q3 earnings results, with revenue rising by 26% and operating margin remaining high at 40%, indicating stable profitability in Meta's core business. Weighing on shares was management's guidance around 2026 expense growth (significantly faster vs. 2025) and capital expenditure growth (meaningfully larger vs. 2025) reducing free cash flow. While Meta's research and development (R&D) and AI spending has increased by about 50% over the last few years, as the company invests aggressively in becoming one of the leaders of the generative AI era, we believe its R&D investments in conjunction with tactical acquisitions such as Manus position Meta for leadership in next-gen technologies. Meta's core digital advertising business remains a cash-generating machine, which positions the company well to bet big on AI and other new businesses.
  • > Shares of enterprise software company Oracle reversed course after rallying in the prior quarter, which stemmed from mounting concerns about the company's margin profile and balance sheet health amid an aggressive push to expand AI data center capacity. It plans to rely on debt financing for this expansion, creating balance sheet risk if key customer OpenAI ultimately requires less compute capacity than anticipated. Shares came under further pressure in mid-December after news broke that Blue Owl Capital withdrew from plans to fund Oracle's multi-billion-dollar data center project in Michigan. However, management later clarified that final negotiations for a financing deal were on schedule with a different equity partner.

Perspective: Vision Before Precision

From the DeepSeek launch to "Liberation Day," 2025 was marked by economic twists and turns few could have predicted; yet, encouragingly, equity markets never fully lost their tether to underlying earnings. Many of the major U.S. market debates of 2025—consumer health, monetary policy, and AI adoption—remain unresolved, and we believe investors could experience a sense of déjà vu in 2026. Specific to AI, economic value has tracked toward vendors fulfilling voracious industry appetite for data center compute capacity. This trend could persist for the near future based on capital expenditure plans at major internet and cloud computing companies. On the other hand, traditional software businesses have yet to buck AI displacement concerns, despite some showing demonstrable progress on AI innovation and monetization. We expect more returns dispersion between perceived AI leaders and laggards this year, underscoring our conviction in a flexible, forward-looking, and all-capitalization investment approach that seeks to navigate the risks of passive market complacency. ZCI's edge continues to be our temperament and time horizon, thanks in large part to our clients who share our conviction that success in growth investing follows rigorous fundamental analysis and is not measured in months or quarters but in years.

data.title: Goldman Sachs International Small Cap Insights Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index returned 2.68% in the fourth quarter of 2025, capping a strong year in 2025. The Index finished the year up 31.83%. Performance in the fourth quarter was supported by a macro backdrop turning more supportive for ex-US equities, value cyclicals outperforming growth, and falling rate expectations alongside foreign exchange moves. Europe and Japan both benefitted from fiscal expansion and easier global financial conditions.

Performance Review

In the fourth quarter of 2025, the Goldman Sachs International Small Cap Insights Fund performed strongly, returning 4.95% and outperforming its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index, by 183 basis points (net, I-share). The Fund experienced positive excess returns each month, with the strongest month of outperformance being October.

From a factor perspective, our Themes & Trends investment pillar was the strongest contributor to excess returns in the quarter. Our signals seeking to assess industry momentum guided overweight positions within the Materials sector. Our overweight positioning towards these names proved additive to excess returns due to the broad trend of Mining stocks outperforming as gold and copper prices rose.

Our Sentiment Analysis pillar was the second largest contributor to performance in the period. Our signals focused on using natural language processing to gauge analyst and management sentiment led to select overweight positioning within the Machinery and Electrical Equipment industries, specifically companies that outperformed due to strong earnings and demand for alternative energy products.

The Fundamental Mispricings pillar also contributed positively to performance. Our suite of signals seeking to compare industry specific valuation metrics were able to guide overweight positioning in select oil & gas companies. This positioning contributed to performance as floating solutions for offshore oil gained amid geopolitical uncertainty.

Finally, our High-Quality Business Models pillar was also additive to performance, although its contribution was more modest. Select underweight positions within Interactive Media & Services had strong contributions to returns due to investor skepticism on AI spending.

data.title: ClearBridge Dividend Strategy ESG Portfolios Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: 2025 marked the third consecutive year of this AI-driven cycle. The market-cap-weighted S&P 500 Index rose 17.9% in the year, while the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index gained just 11.4%. The Magnificent Seven, this market's nucleus, rose 24.9% in 2025. While performance comparisons will vary based on the time period, cap-weighted and equal-weighted indexes have produced similar returns over long periods of time.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in consumer staples and utilities, a health care overweight and IT underweight proved beneficial.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in the communication services, IT, energy and real estate sectors detracted.
  • Outlook: We anticipate the AI debate will carry over in 2026 and we will continue to participate in AI in a measured and disciplined way. Yet, as other investors continue to myopically focus on AI, we expect to find additional idiosyncratic opportunities in overlooked corners of the market. We believe these seeds will bear fruit in the years to come.

Performance Review

  • The S&P 500 managed a 2.7% gain in the fourth quarter, while the Strategy captured roughly two-thirds of that gross of fees. The Strategy underperformed the S&P 500 for the quarter in net of fees.
  • On the plus side, consumer staples holdings such as Nestle, Coca-Cola (KO) and Unilever and a health care overweight shined as concerns of a tech bubble grew.
  • On the downside, we are underweight Alphabet and Apple (AAPL), two Magnificent Seven stocks that performed well, and overweight Oracle (ORCL), which gave back some of its large gains from earlier in 2025. Increasing competitive dynamics in the wireless industry also weighed on T-Mobile.
  • 2025 marked the third consecutive year of this AI-driven cycle. The market-cap-weighted S&P 500 Index rose 17.9% in the year, while the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index gained just 11.4%. The Magnificent Seven, this market's nucleus, rose 24.9% in 2025. Since ChatGPT launched three years ago, the cap-weighted S&P 500 has delivered nearly twice the gains of its equal weighted peer. The Magnificent Seven, meanwhile, has surged a staggering 332%.
  • While performance comparisons will vary based on the time period, cap-weighted and equal-weighted indexes have produced similar returns over long periods of time.
  • In 2025, and throughout the last three years, the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy ESG has enjoyed healthy gains, in part due to its investments in AI participants like Alphabet, Broadcom, Microsoft and Oracle.
  • We have not gained as much as the cap-weighted S&P 500, our benchmark, however, as we maintain diversification to prevent the portfolio from becoming overly concentrated.
  • Market conditions in 2025 provided opportunities in companies we have long followed and admired such as: Automatic Data Processing (ADP) (a fourth-quarter addition), Inditex, Marsh & McLennan (MRSH), Old Dominion Freight Lines (ODFL) and TE Connectivity (TEL). We also took advantage of situations in select existing holdings to significantly increase our positions in Air Products, Texas Instruments and Union Pacific. All represent high-quality franchises in attractive industries underwritten based on reasonable cash returns. It is an eclectic bunch, sourced from bottom-up analysis, not a trend-following, top-down approach.
  • Given our approach to investing — conservative, diversified and risk-averse — AI is not central to our portfolio. Indeed, no one sector or trend will ever be central to the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy ESG.
  • In momentum-driven, concentrated markets, diversification inherently impacts relative performance comparisons. Of course, it also limits losses when that trade, inevitably, unwinds.

Outlook

  • We anticipate the AI debate will carry over in 2026 and we will continue to participate in AI in a measured and disciplined way. Yet, as other investors continue to myopically focus on AI, we expect to find additional idiosyncratic opportunities in overlooked corners of the market. We believe these seeds will bear fruit in the years to come. The Strategy, meanwhile, continues to trade at a significant discount to the broader market while exhibiting robust dividend growth on average. With market concentration and valuations at or near all-time highs, we remain committed to diversification and valuation discipline. These principles have served investors well in the past; we believe they will continue to serve us well in the years ahead.
